coredata.dc:title: Copper-ion-complexed palygorskite as a novel nanozyme with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for sensitive colorimetric sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Ping
Zhong, Hui
Li, Xiao-rong
Zhang, Tingting
Wang, Xiang
Hu, Fang
Yan, Yubo
Xu, Jiaxing
Li, Meisheng
coredata.dc:description: A novel nanozyme, based on copper-ion-complexed palygorskite (PalCu2+), was synthesized using a facile method. The introduction of Cu2+ demonstrates a synergistic effect with Pal, enhancing the peroxidase-like activity for the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2. Steady-state kinetic analysis revealed that PalCu2+ exhibits a higher affinity for substrates compared to native horseradish peroxidase, presenting a practical alternative to natural peroxidase. The efficacy of PalCu2+ as a peroxidase mimic for H2O2 sensing was investigated through colorimetric methods. Additionally, when combined with enzymatic glucose oxidation, a sensitive and selective colorimetric strategy for glucose detection was established. The developed method was effectively applied to detect H2O2 and glucose in real samples. The proposed strategy offers an approach for the rational design and development of metal ion composites, opening new way for future applications of Pal-based materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Palygorskite
Nanozymes
Peroxidase mimics
Copper ions
Colorimetric sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the peroxidase-like activity of PalCu2+ and steady-state kinetics assay
Detection of H2O2 and glucose using PalCu2+ as peroxidase mimics
Detection of H2O2 and glucose using PalCu2+ as a peroxidase mimic

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and compositional characterizations of PalCu2+. Representative (A) TEM image, (B, C) SEM images at different magnifications, and (D) the corresponding EDX mapping image for Cu elements. Scale bars: 1 μm.

FT-IR spectra (A), XRD patterns (B), XPS survey spectra (C), high-resolution XPS Cu 2p spectra of PalCu2+ (D), N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms (E), and the corresponding pore diameter distribution plots (F).

(A) Typical UV − vis absorption spectra of TMB (1) and its one-electron oxidation product (2) catalyzed by PalCu2 + in the presence of H2O2, and corresponding two-electron oxidation product (3) resulting from continued addition of H2SO4. Inset: Chromogenic reaction routes (Zhang et al., 2020b) of TMB and photographs of its corresponding oxidation products upon one-electron and two-electron oxidation. (B) Absorption spectra and photographs of TMB in different reaction systems after stopping the reaction with an equal volume of 2 M H2SO4: (a) TMB + Pal−Cu2+ + H2O2, (b) TMB + Pal + H2O2, (c) TMB + Cu2+ + H2O2, (d) TMB + H2O2, and (e) TMB. Reaction condition: 100 μM TMB, 1 mg mL−1 PalCu2+, 25 mM H2O2, 10 mM HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0).

Steady-state kinetic measurement of the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of PalCu2+. (A) The concentration of H2O2 was kept constant at 0.25 M, while the concentration of TMB was varied. (B) The concentration of TMB was fixed at 100 μM, and the concentration of H2O2 was varied.

Fluorescence emission spectra of TA in the presence of H2O2 and various concentrations of PalCu2+. Reaction condition: 0.5 mM terephthalic acid, 10 mM H2O2, and 10 mM HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0). The fluorescence excitation wavelength was fixed at 317 nm.

(A) UV − vis absorption spectra of the PalCu2+ based sensing system with varying H2O2 concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, and 1000 μM) are depicted from bottom to top. Inset: Photographs corresponding to the respective samples. (B) The absorbance at 450 nm of the sensing system in the presence of different H2O2 concentrations, along with the corresponding linear regression plots. (C) UV − vis absorption spectra of the detection system at different glucose concentrations (down-to-up: 80, 90, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600, and 700 μM). Inset: Photographs of the respective samples. (D) The relationship between the absorbance at 450 nm and glucose concentration, illustrated with the corresponding linear regression curve.
Specificity analysis of the glucose sensing method based on a combination of GOx and PalCu2+. Inset: Photographs of the respective detection solutions.
Schematic representation of the assay procedure.

head.ce:title: Copper-ion-complexed palygorskite as a novel nanozyme with enhanced peroxidase-like activity for sensitive colorimetric sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Engineering Laboratory for Environmental Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biomass-Based Energy and Enzyme Technology, Jiangsu Key Lab for Chemistry of Low-Dimensional Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huaiyin Normal University, Huaian 223300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Engineering Laboratory for Environmental Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biomass-Based Energy and Enzyme Technology, Jiangsu Key Lab for Chemistry of Low-Dimensional Materials
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Engineering Laboratory for Environmental Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Biomass-Based Energy and Enzyme Technology, Jiangsu Key Lab for Chemistry of Low-Dimensional Materials, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huaiyin Normal University, Huaian 223300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nanozyme, based on copper-ion-complexed palygorskite (PalCu2+), was synthesized using a facile method. The introduction of Cu2+ demonstrates a synergistic effect with Pal, enhancing the peroxidase-like activity for the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2. Steady-state kinetic analysis revealed that PalCu2+ exhibits a higher affinity for substrates compared to native horseradish peroxidase, presenting a practical alternative to natural peroxidase. The efficacy of PalCu2+ as a peroxidase mimic for H2O2 sensing was investigated through colorimetric methods. Additionally, when combined with enzymatic glucose oxidation, a sensitive and selective colorimetric strategy for glucose detection was established. The developed method was effectively applied to detect H2O2 and glucose in real samples. The proposed strategy offers an approach for the rational design and development of metal ion composites, opening new way for future applications of Pal-based materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes, a novel class of nanomaterials possessing biocatalytic activities similar to natural enzymes, have emerged as promising alternatives to traditional enzymes due to their inherent traits, including versatile designability, adjustable catalytic performance, adaptability to diverse environments, and cost-effective synthesis (Huang et al., 2019b; Wu et al., 2019). Among the various nanozyme platforms, those based on clay materials have garnered significant interest in recent years (Chen et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023), offering distinct advantages over other nanomaterials. Clay minerals possess high surface areas, abundant surface functional groups, and excellent biocompatibility, making them an ideal platform for constructing nanozyme systems. The porous structure of clay materials facilitates efficient substrate diffusion and enhances the accessibility of active sites, leading to improved catalytic efficiency (Aranda and Ruiz-Hitzky, 2018). Moreover, the scalability and cost-effectiveness of clay materials make them attractive candidates for large-scale production and industrial applications (Ruiz et al., 2023). The abundance of clay minerals in nature, coupled with their low cost and ease of functionalization, further contribute to the economic viability and sustainability of clay-based nanozymes.

Copper serves as a vital micronutrient, participating in various metabolic processes as a crucial enzymatic cofactor. Numerous enzymes in the human body incorporate copper as a redox-active cofactor, playing pivotal roles in energy production, antioxidant defense, neurotransmitter metabolism, and signal transduction (Barnham and Bush, 2014; Guengerich, 2018; Rae et al., 1999). Recently, copper-based nanozymes, encompassing copper-nucleotide complexes (Huang et al., 2019a; Liang et al., 2017; Tran et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2021a), copper-peptide coordination compounds (Rashtbari and Dehghan, 2021; Wang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021b; Zhang et al., 2020a; Zhang et al., 2021), and other copper-containing nanomaterials (Chen et al., 2018; Feng et al., 2022; Jain et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2015; Vázquez-González et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017), have emerged as mimics of natural enzymes, finding extensive applications across various fields. Nevertheless, the commonly used copper supports in the construction of nanozymes, such as carbon-based nanomaterials (Villalba-Rodríguez et al., 2023), involve complex synthesis, high cost, and high energy consumption. Consequently, the search of economically viable and environmentally sustainable carriers for copper remains a challenge in the development of sustainable nanozymes.

Palygorskite (Pal) is a natural, hydrous, magnesium-rich aluminosilicate characterized by a microfibrous crystalloid morphology. The abundance and wide availability of Pal deposits make it an economically viable and sustainable alternative to other substrate materials for catalyst support (Wang and Wang, 2019). The environmental friendliness and biocompatibility of Pal further highlight its potential as a promising platform for developing efficient nanozyme systems. Specifically, the highly dispersed Pal nano-rods, with their large surface area and high surface activity, making them ideal carriers for constructing functional nanocomposites (Yang and Wang, 2022). Various active components have been loaded onto Pal to create a variety of catalysts (He and Yang, 2014; Mu and Wang, 2015; Wang et al., 2014). Additionally, studies have shown that natural nanominerals, including Pal, possess intrinsic enzyme-like properties (Feng et al., 2021). Therefore, the unique properties and versatility of Pal make it a compelling candidate for advancing nanozyme research. For instance, gold and Co3O4 nanoparticles have been modified onto Pal to create peroxidase-like nanozymes for sensing applications (Chen et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2017). Despite these promising attributes, the utilization of Pal as a substrate for immobilizing Cu2+ ion in catalytic applications is still a relatively emerging area of research. Notably, the adsorption mechanism and antibacterial properties of copper-ion-modified Pal have been thoroughly investigated (Cai and Xue, 2008; Zhao et al., 2006). Given this research background, there is a clear need to further develop its application in the field of nanozymes.

On the other hand, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and glucose are of great importance in the biochemical processes and biomedical diagnosis. H2O2 is intricately linked to intracellular signaling pathways, while glucose serves as a crucial carbon source in the energy-generating pathways of cell metabolism (Rhee, 2006; Zhu and Thompson, 2019). Recognizing the imperative for precise and convenient detection of these compounds, a variety of analytical techniques have been developed (Li et al., 2022; Nichols et al., 2013; Sun and James, 2015), including electrochemistry (Wang, 2008; Zhang and Chen, 2017), chromatography (Pinkernell et al., 1997; Song et al., 2017), spectrophotometry (Liu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2023), chemiluminescence (Jia et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2015), and fluorescence (Hu et al., 2014; Yuan et al., 2015), offering both qualitative and quantitative assessments of H2O2 and glucose. The demand for simple and cost-effective assays with easy-to-read results remains ever-increasing. Such approaches are particular significant in regions with limited access to advanced diagnostic equipment, notably in developing countries. Among the various detection methodologies, colorimetry stands out for its intuitive and visually results. In general, colorimetric biosensing data can be read using a portable instrument or even interpreted with the naked eye.

In this study, we prepared a novel nanozyme by complexing copper ions with Pal using a convenient one-step method under mild conditions. The fibrous morphology of Pal, characterized by needle-like crystals with high aspect ratios, provides a large surface area and numerous active sites. This unique structure facilitates robust interactions between the metal ions and the substrate (Liu et al., 2022), promoting the stability of the nanozyme. Furthermore, the fibrous nature of Pal imparts structural integrity to the nanozyme, enhancing its durability and recyclability. Importantly, the resulting nanozymes exhibit significantly enhanced peroxidase-like activity. Building upon these characteristics, we developed a sensitive colorimetric strategy for the detection of H2O2. Additionally, by combining the nanozyme with glucose oxidase, we constructed a convenient method for visual glucose measurement. The established sensor system was successfully applied to the assessment of H2O2 and glucose in real samples.

A 30% solution of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), copper sulfate pentahydrate, acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), terephthalic acid (TA), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS), glucose, d-Fructose, D-(+)-Maltose monohydrate, D-(+)-Sucrose, and α-Lactose monohydrate were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Glucose oxidase (GOx) was obtained from Yingxin Laboratory Equipment Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The pure Pal utilized in this work was sourced from Xuyi, China. Detailed characterizations of Pal have been provided in our previous work (Chen et al., 2021). All materials were used as received. Deionized water (resistivity >18 MΩ cm) was employed to prepare all the buffers and solutions.

Electron microscopy measurements were carried out using a FEI Quanta 450FEG scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a Hitachi HC-1 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Thermo ARLX'TRA diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source (45 kV, 40 mA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using an ESCALab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific) photoelectron spectrometer with 200 W monochromatic Al Kα radiation. Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) measurements were acquired with a Perkin Elemer Optima 2000DV spectrometer. The Micromeritics 3Flex surface characterization analyzer was employed to acquire the N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of the samples and corresponding pore size distribution. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a 756PC UV visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai Jinghua). Fluorescence emission spectroscopy characterization was performed on an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). The zeta potential were measured on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern).

The synthesis of PalCu2+ was achieved by mixing dispersed Pal and copper sulfate in an aqueous solution. In a typical procedure, 0.895 g Pal was dispersed in 35.8 mL deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, copper sulfate was added to the suspension to reach a final concentration of 1.25 M. Following an overnight incubation with vigorous stirring at room temperature, the solution underwent centrifugation to obtain the crude product. The collected solids were thoroughly washed with deionized water and ethanol to remove any unbound copper ions. The resulting product was then subjected to vacuum drying for 6 h and finely ground using a mortar for subsequent experimental procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the peroxidase-like activity of PalCu2+ and steady-state kinetics assay
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained PalCu2+ was introduced into ultrapure water and subjected to ultrasound dispersion for 2 h. The assessment of peroxidase-like activity was conducted based on the catalytic oxidation of TMB with H2O2. In a typical test, the dispersion of PalCu2+ was added into the TMB/H2O2 substrate solution and incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) for 20 min. The reaction was halted by adding an equal volume of 2 M sulfuric acid (H2SO4), and absorption spectra were measured in the range of 350 to 600 nm. Absorbance values were recorded at a wavelength of 450 nm, and color changes were documented using a digital camera. All measurements were performed in triplicate. The peroxidase-like catalytic activity was further analyzed by fitting the data to the Michaelis−Menten equation: ν = ν max[S]/(K m + [S]), where ν represents the reaction velocity, ν max is the apparent maximal initial velocity, [S] is the substrate concentration, and K m is Michaelis constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 and glucose using PalCu2+ as peroxidase mimics
ce:section.ce:para: In the H2O2 detection experiments, various concentrations of H2O2 were added to solutions containing 10 mM HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0), 0.2 mM TMB, and 1 mg mL−1 PalCu2+. The resulting detection solutions were thoroughly mixed using a vortex mixer and incubated at room temperature before measuring absorption spectra and capturing photographs. In a typical glucose assay, 50 μL of a solution containing GOx (500 u mL−1) and various concentrations of glucose was incubated at room temperature in the HAc-NaAc buffer for 30 min. Subsequently, 20 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (100 mM, pH 4.0), 20 μL of PalCu2+ (10 mg mL−1), 10 μL of TMB (2 mM), and 150 μL of H2O were added to the aforementioned solution. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 60 min, and absorption spectra were measured after adding an equal volume of 2 M H2SO4. In real sample analyses, milk samples were purchased from a local supermarket, and urine was sampled from a healthy volunteer. The raw samples were centrifuged at 16,500 rpm for 60 min. The supernatant was diluted with deionized water and further centrifuged twice to remove possible interfering substances. After filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane, an appropriate amount of the final solution was added to the detection system, and colorimetric analysis was performed under optimized conditions.

The pure Pal was initially dispersed into an aqueous solution using an ultrasonic method. Subsequently, the dispersed Pal was coordinated with dissolved Cu2+ ions to obtain copper-ion-modified Pal. The morphology of PalCu2+ was characterized through TEM and SEM measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 1A and B, PalCu2+ maintains a rod-like structure, similar to that of pristine Pal (Fig. S1). The large-field SEM image of PalCu2+ and its corresponding elemental mapping analysis demonstrate that Cu elements are evenly distributed throughout the sample (Fig. 1C and D). According to the ICP analysis, the amount of adsorbed Cu2+ on Pal is 7 mg/g. Additionally, the energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra before and after copper modification further confirm the successful uniform incorporation of Cu to Pal (Fig. S2).

We conducted a comparative analysis of the infrared spectra of Pal loaded with copper(II) ions and the original Pal. As shown in Fig. 2A, no discernible difference is evident between the copper-modified Pal and the pristine Pal in the characteristic frequency region. Specifically, the broad bands observed in the high-frequency region between 3640 and 3200 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching modes of the coordinated and zeolitic water molecules. The strong bands at 3549 and 3416 cm−1 correspond to the symmetric and antisymmetric stretching vibrations of the water molecules. The O − H bending modes of the water and clay lattices concentrate around 1654 and 1438 cm−1. The three bands located at 1194, 1028, and 986 cm−1 are attributed to the Si − O − Si stretching vibrations in the silicate tetrahedron. These results confirm that the basic structure of Pal can still be maintained after Cu(II) ion modification. Furthermore, two new bands at 796 and 758 cm−1 are observed in PalCu2+ but are not present in bare Pal. These bands likely correspond to the bending vibrations of Si − OCu, suggesting that copper cations are loaded at energetically favored sites with the formation of coordination bonds. Additionally, a series of band changes occur in the range from 600 to 400 cm−1, which should be due to the vibrations of Cu − O from the bonding between copper and Pal. The coordination complexes involve the sharing of electron pairs between the copper(II) ions and the surface functional groups, leading to stable adsorption.

The XRD patterns of the samples (Fig. 2B) exhibit characteristic reflections at 8.3°,13.7°, 19.8° and 27.5° from the (1 1 0), (2 0 0), (0 4 0) and (4 0 0) planes of crystalline Pal. Even after modification with Cu2+ ions, the samples maintain the typical reflections of Pal, with no noticeable change in the intensity of the diffraction peak corresponding to each Pal basal plane. This consistency strongly suggests that the crystalline structure of Pal remains unaltered following Cu2+ adsorption, indicating a high degree of structural integrity. Furthermore, the absence of X-ray signals corresponding to Cu oxides after Cu2+ loading is significant. This suggests that either no sharp crystalline phases of Cu oxide nanoclusters formed on the surface of Pal during the adsorption process, or their quantity is below the detection limit of XRD (Chen et al., 2019). When combined with the aforementioned infrared characterization results, this indicates that copper ions have an affinity for the dangling oxygen, potentially leading to the formation of [−Si (or Al) − O − Cu] complexes on the tetrahedral sheet of Pal (Cai and Xue, 2008). Further techniques are needed to identify the valence state of copper in the nanozyme catalyst.

Subsequently, we conducted XPS surveys to reveal the surface composition, electron interactions and element chemical states of the samples. In comparison to pristine Pal, the XPS scan of the as-synthesized PalCu2+ reveals two Cu 2p peaks centered at approximately 954 and 934 eV, corresponding to the Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 binding energies, respectively (Fig. 2C and Fig. S3). This observation illustrates the successful loading of copper onto Pal. Moreover, high-resolution XPS spectra of Cu 2p show that the Cu 2p3/2 region of the as-obtained PalCu2+ can be further deconvoluted into two characteristic peaks at 935.2 and 933.0 eV, indicating that copper primarily exists on the Pal surface as Cu(II) species (Fig. 2D) (Chen et al., 2021). Simultaneously, the presence of two shake-up satellites located at 943.1 and 963.2 eV on the high-energy side of the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 peaks, respectively, can be attributed to the charge transfer between Cu 3d orbitals and the surrounding ligand O 2p orbitals (Li et al., 2016). These results suggest the existence of a significant interaction between the doped copper ions and the supporting Pal substrate, consistent with the FT-IR analysis above.

The N2 adsorption-desorption curves of the obtained samples conform to type II isotherms (Fig. 2E). The BET specific surface area and BJH adsorption cumulative pore volume of the Cu2+-modified Pal are 134 m2 g−1 and 0.437 cm3 g−1, respectively, while those of the pristine Pal prior to the modification with Cu2+ are 160 m2 g−1 and 0.476 cm3 g−1, respectively (Fig. 2F). The reduction in specific surface area and cumulative pore volume of Pal treated with Cu2+ is consistent with the effective incorporation of Cu2+ to Pal. Meanwhile, Pal possesses a high surface area and a porous structure (Suárez et al., 2022), providing ample sites for Cu2+ ions to adsorb. The accessibility of these surface sites enhances the adsorption capacity of Pal for Cu2+ ions.

Furthermore, we also examined the zeta potential of the samples to further confirm the modification results. We prepared three samples in pH 7.0 aqueous solution, which included raw Pal (1 mg mL−1), PalCu2+ (1 mg mL−1), and a mixture composed of Pal (1 mg mL−1) and Cu2+ ions (0.11 mM) (with the same copper content as the aforementioned samples). The zeta potential of each of the three samples was then measured at 20 °C. The measured potential value of the Pal solution was −14.07 ± 1.12 mV. Loaded with positively charged copper ions, the resulting PalCu2+ potential value decreased to −3.84 ± 0.23 mV. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of the mixture consisting of Pal and homogeneous Cu2+ ions was −11.97 ± 0.90 mV under the same measurement conditions. This indicates that Cu2+ ions were successfully modified to Pal.

The evaluation of the peroxidase-like activity of PalCu2+ was conducted using TMB as a substrate under optimized reaction conditions. Like natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP), the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of PalCu2+ depends on the pH and temperature. As depicted in Fig. S4A, the nanozyme exhibits high catalytic activity within the pH range of 3.0 to 4.5, which closely resembles the optimal pH range for natural HRP. We selected pH 4.0 as the standard condition for subsequent activity analysis. Furthermore, Fig. S4B demonstrates that PalCu2+ displays significant activity within the normal temperature range (20–40 °C), which is crucial for detection in biological systems. Hence, for convenience, we chose room temperature (∼25 °C) for subsequent detection experiments. Under the optimized conditions mentioned above, the catalytic reaction reaches equilibrium in approximately twenty minutes (Fig. S4C). The chromogenic reaction of TMB involves its oxidation by H2O2 under PalCu2+ catalysis, resulting in the efficient transformation of the colorless substrate into colored oxidized products. Upon the addition of H2SO4 to stop the reaction, the solution changed from blue to yellow, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 3A. These observable color changes were quantified through absorption spectroscopy and were easily discernible to the naked eye (Fig. 3A and Fig. S5A). As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the introduction of PalCu2+ dispersion into the mixture of TMB and H2O2 induced significant alterations in both the color and absorbance of the solution. In contrast, the addition of Pal or Cu2+ individually resulted in minimal changes in solution color and produced relatively low absorbance of the oxidation products, indicating an enhanced peroxidase-like activity of PalCu2+ composites. Additionally, ABTS, another representative peroxidase substrate, was employed to further validate the catalytic activity of PalCu2+. Visual observation and spectroscopic measurements confirmed that PalCu2+ catalyzed the oxidation of ABTS by H2O2, generating ABTS+• (Fig. S5B).

Steady-state kinetic analysis of the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of PalCu2+ revealed that the relationship between initial velocity values and substrate concentrations of TMB or H2O2 followed the typical Michaelis–Menten model. The Michaelis constant (K m) and apparent maximal initial velocity (ν max), estimated by fitting the corresponding data, are presented in Fig. 4 and Table S1. K m is a crucial parameter for evaluating the catalytic activity of the enzyme, with lower K m values indicating stronger affinity between the catalyst and the substrate. The K m values of PalCu2+ with TMB and H2O2 as substrates were determined to be 0.015 and 1.471 mM, respectively. These values were found to be lower than those observed for HRP. This suggests that PalCu2+ exhibits a stronger binding affinity toward the substrates, resulting in a significantly enhanced peroxidase-like activity.

Additionally, we conducted a comparison of the catalytic activity between the leaching solution and intact PalCu2+ in the TMB oxidation reaction. The leaching supernatant, obtained by centrifuging PalCu2+ after a two-hour ultrasound treatment, exhibited weak catalytic activity (Fig. S6). ICP analysis results indicated that <1 % of copper ions were leached from the copper-ion-complexed Pal after the mentioned treatments. This observation is consistent with previous findings reported in the literature (Cai and Xue, 2008). Furthermore, the catalytic activity of PalCu2+ surpassed that of a mixture composed of Pal and homogeneous Cu2+ ions (Fig. S7). These findings suggest that the observed peroxidase-like activity primarily originates from PalCu2+ itself rather than from leached ions. Notably, the relative catalytic activity of PalCu2+ was essentially stabilized at the equilibrium level after treatment at a range of temperatures for one hour, immersion in water for one week, and repeated use for five catalytic cycles, respectively (Fig. S8). In addition, the structural characterization and microstructural features of the catalyst remained unchanged after the catalytic reaction (Fig. S9 and S10). These observations suggest that the stability of PalCu2+ was robust in the corresponding buffer solution system, and the doped copper cations were challenging to leach from PalCu2+ due to the stable coordination of copper ions with Pal substrates. PalCu2+ exhibits good operational stability, effectively preventing the potential release of free copper ions into the environment.

The potential catalytic mechanism of the peroxidase mimics involves the catalysis of hydrogen peroxide by PalCu2+, producing hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These radicals efficiently oxidize TMB, generating the corresponding oxidized products. To confirm the catalytic activation mechanism, we utilized terephthalic acid (TA) as a fluorescent probe to investigate the impact of PalCu2+ on •OH generation. TA reacts with •OH to form 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, yielding highly sensitive fluorescence signals. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity increases gradually with rising PalCu2+ concentration, indicating that PalCu2+ promotes hydroxyl radical production. The proposed catalytic processes are outlined in Eqs. (1)–(3) (Lázaro Martínez et al., 2008):
(1)
Hydrogen peroxide binds to the coordination site of the copper ion of PalCu2+ to form a complex composed of a catalyst and a superoxide.
(2)
The resulting complex then reacts with another H2O2 molecule to produce O2, •OH and H2O.
(3)
PalCu2+ is regenerated by H2O2 with an additional •OH produced.
(1)
Pal −
Cu
2 +
+
H 2
O 2
→ Pal −
Cu +
•
O 2
H +
H +
(2)
Pal −
Cu +
•
O 2
H +
H 2
O 2
→ Pal −
Cu +
+
O 2
+ • OH +
H 2
O
(3)
Pal −
Cu +
+
H 2
O 2
+
H +
→ Pal −
Cu
2 +
+ • OH +
H 2
O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 and glucose using PalCu2+ as a peroxidase mimic
ce:section.ce:para: In light of the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of PalCu2+, we constructed a convenient and sensitive colorimetric sensor for the detection of H2O2 and glucose (Scheme 1 ).

Given that the color variation of the PalCu2+ based detection system is largely dependent on the concentration of H2O2, we developed a colorimetric sensor for H2O2 based on the relationship between H2O2 concentration and UV − vis absorbance at 450 nm. Under optimized conditions, the absorbance and color of the sensing system gradually increased with the H2O2 concentration ranging from 0 to 1 mM (Fig. 6A). As illustrated in Fig. 6B, there is a good linear correlation between the concentration of H2O2 and the absorbance value at 450 nm in two concentration ranges, respectively. The first range spans H2O2 concentrations from 10 to 80 μM, with the linear regression equation expressed as A = 0.0084C
H2O2 + 0.9964, where A is the absorbance intensity at 450 nm of the detection system with different H2O2 concentrations (C
H2O2 ), and the correlation coefficient is 0.9922. The second range covers H2O2 concentrations from 200 to 1000 μM, with the linear regression equation as A = 0.0002C
H2O2 + 1.7983 and a correlation coefficient R
2 = 0.9461. From Fig. 6B and the linear regression equations presented, it is evident that the calibration plot at low concentrations is steeper than that at high concentrations. The disparity in the linear slopes between the two H2O2 concentration ranges may be attributed to the diverse reaction states of the nanozymes with H2O2 (Song et al., 2020). Specifically, at lower concentrations, the added H2O2 can interact more effectively with PalCu2+. Simultaneously, the color change in the reaction solution distinctly observable (Fig. 6A, inset), providing a simple and facile approach for detecting H2O2 at low concentrations. The limit of detection (LOD) for H2O2 is as low as 1.82 μM (3σ/slope).

Moreover, H2O2 is the primary product of glucose oxidation catalyzed by GOx. The production of H2O2 is dependent on the concentration of glucose, making the proposed method capable of determining glucose levels. In Fig. 6C, the UV − vis absorption spectra of the corresponding reaction solutions at various glucose concentrations are depicted. The absorbance intensity increases with the rise in glucose concentrations. Additionally, the color variation in the glucose response is visually apparent and easily distinguishable by the naked eye (Fig. 6C, inset). Therefore, colorimetric glucose detection can be effortlessly achieved. The absorbance value (A) at 450 nm linearly correlates with the glucose concentration (C
glucose ) in the range of 80 to 700 μM (Fig. 6D). The linear regression equation is expressed as A = 0.0007C
glucose + 0.9668, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9847, and the detection limit estimated at 78.87 μM.

The sensor developed above, based on the peroxidase-like activity of PalCu2+, and the representative detection systems for H2O2 and glucose using various nanomaterials and methods, are summarized in Table S2. The linear range and LOD of our strategy are comparable to those reported previously. The results indicate that the established colorimetric method, with good precision, is suitable for the determination of H2O2 and glucose.

To assess the specificity of the colorimetric assay for glucose detection, we conducted control experiments with various saccharides, such as fructose, maltose, lactose, and sucrose, as substitutes for glucose, respectively. As expected, glucose exhibited the highest absorbance intensity at 450 nm compared to the other interfering analytes (Fig. 7 ). The selectivity of the colorimetric method is evident to the naked eye (Fig. 7, inset). Therefore, the established method can serve as a simple and selective strategy for glucose detection.

To confirm the reliability of the Pal−Cu2+-based colorimetric sensor for H2O2 and glucose detection, we assessed the practicality of the established method in real samples. H2O2 is often used as a fresh-keeping agent in commercial dairy products. However, excessive addition of H2O2 to milk can reduce its nutritional value and even pose health risks. Thus, the selective and sensitive detection of H2O2 in milk holds significant importance. Here, the recovery of H2O2 from commercially available milk was determined using a standard addition method based on the sensor mentioned above. The recoveries ranged from 95% to 111%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 0.7% to 3.8% (Tables S3). These results indicate that our method offers satisfactory recovery rates and is suitable for the detection of H2O2 in real samples. Since measurements of glucose in urine is considered a valuable non-invasive method for monitoring diabetes during therapy and can provide complementary information to blood glucose (Lankelma et al., 2012), simple, reliable, and inexpensive methods for urine glucose monitoring are highly desirable during diabetes treatment. Using the detection process through the combination of GOx and PalCu2+, we detected glucose spiked into urine samples with the standard addition method. As shown in the measurement results in Tables S4, the proposed method can satisfy the determination of glucose in urine samples with acceptable precision and repeatability.

In this study, we synthesized copper-ion-complexed Pal using a facile method. The obtained PalCu2+ exhibited intrinsic peroxidase-like activity for the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. A sensitive colorimetric strategy was established to detect H2O2 using PalCu2+ as a peroxidase mimic. Moreover, combined with glucose oxidase, we also describe a selective method for the visual detection of glucose. The developed assay allows the sensing of H2O2 and glucose with low detection limits and wide linear ranges. Our proposed strategy provides an approach for the rational design and development of metal ion composites, opening the way for subsequent applications of Pal-based materials. The introduction of Cu2+ exhibits a synergistic effect with Pal to enhance the peroxidase-like activity, presenting a novel strategy for nanozyme design based on nanoclays with new potential for the future development of enzyme mimics.

Ping Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hui Zhong: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Xiao-rong Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Tingting Zhang: Validation, Formal analysis. Xiang Wang: Validation, Formal analysis. Fang Hu: Formal analysis. Yubo Yan: Supervision, Resources. Jiaxing Xu: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Meisheng Li: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078122 and 21775051).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MnO2-nanosheet-modified upconversion nanosystem for sensitive turn-on fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose in blood
